common_name: Needleroot Airplant Orchid (Florida Needleroot Orchid, Needleroot Epidendrum)

planting.note: Grow mounted on rough bark or cork with the roots exposed to bright indirect light; Harrisella porrecta is a leafless epiphytic orchid that photosynthesizes entirely through its roots — the roots must receive adequate light (bright indirect to partial direct) to support the plant. Extremely slow-growing and highly specialized, this orchid is suited to patient collectors and enthusiasts interested in Florida's rare native flora. Found naturally in Collier and Monroe Counties in Florida on live oak, pop ash, and pond apple trees. Wild plants are protected by Florida law and by CITES — purchase only from nurseries propagating legally. Zone 10-12 outdoors only.

directions: Grow mounted on rough bark with roots exposed to filtered indirect light and high humidity — Needleroot Airplant Orchid is a leafless, photosynthetic-root orchid that subsists entirely on light captured through its chlorophyll-containing roots and nutrients from air moisture and rain, making root light exposure the single most important cultural requirement. Harrisella porrecta is one of the tiniest orchids native to North America — a minute, completely leafless epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family found in southern Florida (Collier and Monroe Counties, including the Big Cypress and Florida Keys) and throughout the Caribbean, Mexico, and Central America. Like the more famous Ghost Orchid (Dendrophylax lindenii) and the related Campylocentrum micranthum, Harrisella porrecta has eliminated its leaves entirely and depends on flat, strap-like, gray-green roots that cling to tree bark surfaces for all photosynthesis. The plant itself is nearly invisible on its host tree — appearing as a few thin, spreading, flatly-appressed roots spreading across bark, indistinguishable from a lichen or bark mark to the untrained eye. In summer and fall, tiny flower spikes (0.5–1 inch) emerge from the root nodes, bearing 3–8 minute (under 0.25 inch) flowers in pale yellow-green with a white lip — flowers that require a hand lens to fully appreciate but are perfectly formed in miniature. Harrisella porrecta is one of the rarest and most specialized of Florida's native orchids, of genuine interest to native plant specialists and orchid collectors fascinated by the biology of leafless, root-photosynthesizing orchids. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Harrisella porrecta: Florida native leafless orchid
Florida Native Plant Society — Harrisella porrecta species profile
American Orchid Society — Leafless Florida orchids: Harrisella, Campylocentrum, and Ghost Orchid
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Native Orchids of Florida

common_name: Mahogany Fern (Maidenhair Tree Fern, Hump Fern, Truncate Didymochlaena)

planting.note: Plant in deep to partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil; Didymochlaena truncatula is one of the most elegant and distinctive shade ferns for zone 10-12 outdoor use or a shaded indoor container. The new fronds emerge in rich copper-pink to bronze-mahogany color before maturing to glossy dark green — the color sequence from which the common name 'Mahogany Fern' derives. Sensitive to temperatures below 45°F; bring indoors or protect in zone 9. Water consistently — allow the medium to approach dryness between waterings but never fully dry. Slow to establish but long-lived once settled. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in deep to partial shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil — Mahogany Fern grows most beautifully in the cool, moist, deeply shaded conditions of a sheltered porch, conservatory, or jungle garden in zone 10-12 and is one of the most elegant of all shade ferns when well-established. Didymochlaena truncatula is a large, upright-growing terrestrial fern in the Didymochlaenaceae family (sometimes placed in Dryopteridaceae) with a broadly pan-tropical distribution — native to moist forests in tropical Africa, Madagascar, Asia, Australia, the Pacific Islands, the Caribbean, and tropical Americas. The plant produces a broad, erect rosette of large, bipinnate fronds 24–48 inches long, each frond having a distinctive architectural quality: the individual pinnules (small leaflets) are oblong with truncated (squared-off) bases and tips, giving the frond a clean, precise, almost geometric appearance unlike the delicate filigree of most ferns. The young fronds emerging from the crown are the plant's most spectacular feature — they emerge in rich mahogany-bronze to coppery-pink, a color that glows in shaded light before gradually maturing to glossy, dark forest-green. The contrast between the soft, metallic young fronds and the dark mature fronds creates a year-round multi-tonal display. The trunk gradually develops a short, upright base over many years, giving old specimens a tree-fern-like quality. An elegant, distinctive shade fern of botanical interest. Non-toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Didymochlaena truncatula species profile
American Fern Society — Didymochlaena truncatula: Mahogany Fern cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Shade ferns for Florida interiors and zone 10-12 gardens
Florida Native Plant Society — Tropical shade ferns for zone 10 Florida

common_name: Peanut Butter Fruit (Amazon Peanut Butter Tree, Peanut Butter Plant)

planting.note: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Bunchosia glandulifera is a compact, evergreen tropical shrub-tree that produces small oval fruits turning from green to orange to deep red when ripe — the ripe flesh has a thick, sweet, pasty, distinctly peanut-butter-like flavor that makes this one of the most unique subtropical fruits. Excellent in smoothies, eaten fresh, or used as a sweet spread. Very productive in zone 9b-12; in zone 9, established plants cut back in freezes but regrow from the base. Propagate from seed (germinates in 2-4 weeks) or cuttings. Best harvested when the fruit turns fully orange-red and yields to gentle pressure.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Peanut Butter Fruit is adaptable to a range of soil types and is moderately drought tolerant once established, performing reliably in zone 9b-12 as an evergreen fruiting shrub-tree. Bunchosia glandulifera is an evergreen to semi-deciduous, large shrub to small tree native to Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Venezuela, and the Amazon basin in the Malpighiaceae family — the same family as Acerola (Malpighia emarginata), from which the distinctive wing-petaled yellow flowers of Bunchosia are recognizable. The plant produces ovate, dark-green, glossy leaves and clusters of small (0.5-inch) yellow flowers with the characteristic wing-petaled (fringed petal margin) form typical of the Malpighiaceae. The flowers are followed by oval fruits, 1–1.5 inches long, that ripen progressively from pale green through yellow-orange to deep red-orange. The ripe flesh of Bunchosia glandulifera fruit is thick, sweet, slightly dry in texture, and has a flavor that is remarkably and unmistakably reminiscent of smooth peanut butter — sweet, rich, and nutty, without any actual peanut content. The fruit can be eaten fresh at the ripe orange-red stage (before it becomes overripe), blended into smoothies, used as a natural sweet spread on crackers, or incorporated into desserts. Plants are highly productive in warm climates and begin fruiting within 1–2 years from a large transplant or seedling. An unusual and genuinely delicious tropical fruit for zone 9b-12 gardens. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bunchosia glandulifera: Peanut Butter Fruit for Florida
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Tropical Malpighiaceae for Florida and Gulf Coast cultivation
ECHO Development Notes — Bunchosia glandulifera: peanut butter fruit tree for the tropics
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Rare Tropical Fruits: Bunchosia species

common_name: Beach Jack Bean (Bay Bean, Seaside Jack Bean, Coastal Jack Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow in full sun in any well-drained, sandy, coastal soil; Canavalia rosea is the standard beach-stabilizing, nitrogen-fixing legume of tropical coastlines worldwide and the default choice for establishing live cover on coastal dunes, sand flats, and disturbed coastal ground in zone 9-12. The large, showy pink to rose-purple flowers are produced continuously through the warm months and are attractive to large native bees. The large (3–5 inch) flat beans are TOXIC when raw and should not be eaten without extensive preparation — this is a cover crop and coastal stabilizer, not a culinary bean. Non-toxic external contact.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in any well-drained, sandy, coastal soil — Beach Jack Bean is one of the most drought and salt tolerant leguminous plants available, thriving in the harsh conditions of coastal dunes, beach margins, and sandy disturbed ground where most other plants fail to establish. Canavalia rosea is a vigorous, perennial, trailing to sprawling vine in the Fabaceae family with a circumtropical distribution on the coastlines of both hemispheres, naturally occurring on beaches from Florida's Gulf and Atlantic coasts through the Caribbean, Mexico, Central and South America, tropical Africa, India, Southeast Asia, and Australia — one of the most cosmopolitan coastal plants in the world. The plant produces trifoliate leaves with large, oval, dark-green leaflets on trailing stems that spread across the sand surface, rooting at nodes to form a dense, stabilizing mat 12–18 inches deep. Through the warm months, erect flower racemes emerge bearing 5–15 large (1-inch), showy flowers in bright rose-pink to purple-pink — among the most ornamental of any legume flower. The flowers attract large carpenter bees and other native bees. After flowering, large, flat, rigid pods 3–5 inches long develop containing the large, kidney-shaped seeds. Beach Jack Bean fixes nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis and adds organic matter as stems root and die, steadily improving poor coastal soils. The beans contain toxic alkaloids (canavanine) and are poisonous when raw — not for culinary use without extensive processing. An outstanding, low-maintenance native legume for coastal dune stabilization and cover cropping in zone 9-12.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Canavalia rosea: Bay Bean for Florida coastal dune restoration
Florida Native Plant Society — Canavalia rosea (Beach Jack Bean) species profile
USDA NRCS Plant Materials Center — Canavalia rosea for coastal dune stabilization
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Coastal cover crops for Gulf Coast zone 9 landscapes

common_name: Purple Tephrosia (Wild Indigo, Fish Poison Plant, Wild Hog Weed)

planting.note: Direct sow in full sun in any lean, well-drained, sandy to loamy soil; Tephrosia purpurea is a fast-growing, nitrogen-fixing, drought-tolerant shrubby legume native to tropical Asia, widely naturalized throughout the Gulf Coast and Caribbean, and an important larval host plant for the Ceraunus Blue butterfly (Hemiargus ceraunus) throughout zone 9-12. It grows naturally in disturbed, sandy, open ground and establishes rapidly in poor soils. The small pink to purple flowers are produced prolifically from spring through fall. CAUTION: Tephrosia purpurea contains rotenone and related toxins and is poisonous to fish and mildly toxic to mammals if consumed in quantity — do not plant near fish ponds or allow livestock to graze.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in any lean, well-drained, sandy soil — Purple Tephrosia establishes readily in poor, disturbed, sandy soils and is one of the most drought-tolerant and ecologically valuable small legumes for zone 9-12, functioning as a nitrogen fixer, butterfly larval host, and pollinator plant simultaneously. Tephrosia purpurea is a low, spreading, semi-woody annual to short-lived perennial shrub in the Fabaceae family (tribe Millettieae) native to tropical Asia, Africa, and Australia, now naturalized throughout the Caribbean, Florida, and the Gulf Coast in open, disturbed, sunny habitats. The plant produces upright to spreading stems bearing pinnately compound leaves of 9–17 pairs of narrow, oblong, gray-green leaflets, and through the warm months produces numerous small (0.3–0.5 inch) pink to rosy-purple typical legume flowers in axillary and terminal racemes — modest individually but produced in sufficient quantity to provide a continuous, soft display through the growing season. As a legume, Tephrosia purpurea fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis and improves sandy, depleted soils over time. Its ecological role as a larval host plant for the Ceraunus Blue butterfly (Hemiargus ceraunus) throughout south Florida and the Caribbean makes it particularly valuable for butterfly gardens in zone 9b-12, where this small, brilliant blue butterfly seeks out Tephrosia for egg-laying. The plant also contains rotenone-related compounds that were historically used as a fish poison and mild insecticide — do not plant near fish ponds.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tephrosia purpurea for Florida butterfly gardens
Florida Native Plant Society — Tephrosia purpurea species profile
Florida Museum of Natural History — Ceraunus Blue butterfly host plants: Tephrosia
USDA NRCS Plant Materials — Tephrosia purpurea for Gulf Coast cover crop use
